content:
# Exodus Chapter 11 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~38r-38v (PDF pp. 72-73); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 51-52 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_011_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_011_page_002.jpg`

---

## Announcement of the Final Plague -- Nega Echad (vv. 1-10)

**11:1** And YHWH said to Moses, "One more plague I will bring upon Pharaoh and upon Egypt. Afterward he will send you out from here. When he sends you out completely, driving, he will drive you out from here. [fn1]

**11:2** Speak, please, in the ears of the people, and let them ask, each man from his neighbor and each woman from her neighbor, articles of silver and articles of gold." [fn2]

**11:3** And YHWH gave the people favor in the eyes of Egypt. Also the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt, in the eyes of the servants of Pharaoh and in the eyes of the people. [fn3]

**11:4** And Moses said, "Thus says YHWH: 'About midnight I am going out into the midst of Egypt. [fn4]

**11:5** And every firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sits upon his throne to the firstborn of the maidservant who is behind the millstones, and every firstborn of the livestock. [fn5]

**11:6** And there shall be a great cry in all the land of Egypt, such as has never been and such as shall never be again. [fn6]

**11:7** But against all the sons of Israel not a dog shall sharpen its tongue, from man to beast -- so that you may know that YHWH makes a distinction between Egypt and between Israel.' [fn7]

**11:8** And all these your servants shall come down to me and bow down to me, saying, 'Go out, you and all the people who are at your feet.' And afterward I will go out." And he went out from Pharaoh in burning anger. [fn8]

**11:9** And YHWH said to Moses, "Pharaoh will not listen to you, so that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt." [fn9]

> **11:10** **And Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh. And YHWH strengthened [chazaq, PIEL] the heart of Pharaoh, and he did not send out the sons of Israel from his land.** [fn10]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Nega echad* and the infinitive absolute *garesh yegaresh*: "driving, he will drive you out"** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh od nega echad avi al-Par'oh ve'al-Mitsrayim akharei-khen yeshalakh etkhem mizzeh keshallekho kalah garesh yegaresh etkhem mizzeh* (וַיֹּ֨אמֶר יְהוָ֜ה אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֗ה ע֣וֹד נֶ֤גַע אֶחָד֙ אָבִ֤יא עַל־פַּרְעֹה֙ וְעַל־מִצְרַ֔יִם אַחֲרֵי־כֵ֕ן יְשַׁלַּ֥ח אֶתְכֶ֖ם מִזֶּ֑ה כְּשַׁלְּח֣וֹ כָּלָ֔ה גָּרֵ֥שׁ יְגָרֵ֛שׁ אֶתְכֶ֖ם מִזֶּֽה). The noun *nega* ("plague, blow, stroke") from the root *naga* ("to touch, strike") differs from the more common *makkah*; it conveys a divine blow, a striking touch. The numeral *echad* ("one") underscores finality -- precisely one decisive blow remains. The infinitive absolute construction *garesh yegaresh* is the critical translation decision for this verse. EMET renders "he will surely drive you out," collapsing the infinitive absolute into the adverb "surely." LOGOS likewise renders "he will surely drive you out." PNEUMA renders "he will drive you out completely, as one drives out an unwanted guest" -- capturing the force but adding an interpretive simile absent from the Hebrew. The SOPHIA convention requires preserving the infinitive absolute emphasis pattern. The consensus therefore renders "driving, he will drive you out" -- the participial form preserves the Hebrew doubling structure (*garesh yegaresh*) while remaining intelligible in English. The verb *garash* ("to drive out, to expel") echoes 10:11 where Pharaoh drove Moses from his presence; now Pharaoh will drive all Israel from his land. The shift from *shalakh* ("send out") to *garash* ("drive out") signals the reversal: the one who refused to send will end by forcibly expelling. LXX (Vaticanus): *eti mian plegen epaxo epi Pharao kai ep' Aigupton* -- *plege* ("blow, plague") for *nega*.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Sha'al*: "ask" (NOT "borrow")** -- Hebrew: *dabber-na be'oznei ha'am veyish'alu ish me'et re'ehu ve'ishah me'et re'utah kelei-khesef ukhelei zahav* (דַּבֶּר־נָ֘א בְּאָזְנֵ֣י הָעָם֒ וְיִשְׁאֲל֞וּ אִ֣ישׁ מֵאֵ֣ת רֵעֵ֗הוּ וְאִשָּׁה֙ מֵאֵ֣ת רְעוּתָ֔הּ כְּלֵי־כֶ֖סֶף וּכְלֵ֥י זָהָֽב). All three translators agree: the verb *sha'al* means "to ask, to request" -- NOT "to borrow" (as in the KJV and many traditional renderings). There is no obligation of return implied in the Hebrew verb. The Israelites are instructed to ask their Egyptian neighbors for articles of silver and gold, and the context of v. 3 (YHWH gives favor) makes clear the Egyptians will give willingly. This fulfills the promise to Abraham in Genesis 15:14: "afterward they shall come out with great substance." The word *kelei* ("articles, vessels, implements") from *keli* designates portable valuables. The phrase *be'oznei ha'am* ("in the ears of the people") preserves the oral-culture idiom. EMET: "Speak, please, in the ears of the people, and let them ask"; LOGOS: "Speak now in the ears of the people, and let them ask"; PNEUMA: "Tell the people now: every man is to ask." The consensus follows EMET's rendering of *dabber-na* as "speak, please" -- preserving *na* as a polite particle ("please") rather than the temporal "now" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). LXX: *aitesato* ("let each one ask/request") -- *aiteo* confirms the non-deceptive meaning.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Chen be'einei Mitsrayim*: "favor in the eyes of Egypt"** -- Hebrew: *vayyitten YHWH et-chen ha'am be'einei Mitsrayim gam ha'ish Mosheh gadol me'od be'erets Mitsrayim be'einei avdei-Par'oh uve'einei ha'am* (וַיִּתֵּ֧ן יְהוָ֛ה אֶת־חֵ֥ן הָעָ֖ם בְּעֵינֵ֣י מִצְרָ֑יִם גַּ֣ם הָאִ֣ישׁ מֹשֶׁ֡ה גָּדוֹל֩ מְאֹ֨ד בְּאֶ֤רֶץ מִצְרַ֨יִם֙ בְּעֵינֵ֣י עַבְדֵי־פַרְעֹ֔ה וּבְעֵינֵ֖י הָעָֽם). The noun *chen* ("favor, grace, charm") is divinely bestowed -- YHWH engineers Egyptian goodwill toward Israel. The phrase *be'einei* ("in the eyes of") appears three times, marking a shift in the Egyptian gaze from contempt to awe. The parenthetical aside -- *gam ha'ish Mosheh gadol me'od* ("also the man Moses was very great") -- is a rare editorial insertion within a divine speech context. The construction *ha'ish Mosheh* ("the man Moses") is both humanizing and reverential: the shepherd-stutterer of Exodus 4:10 is now *gadol me'od* in the empire. EMET: "YHWH gave the chen [favor/grace] of the people in the eyes of Mitsrayim"; LOGOS: "the LORD gave the people favor in the eyes of Egypt"; PNEUMA: "YHWH caused the Egyptians to look with favor on the people." EMET preserves the Hebrew syntax most literally but adds a bracketed gloss for *chen*. PNEUMA restructures the sentence entirely, making Egyptians the subject. The consensus follows LOGOS's rendering -- "YHWH gave the people favor in the eyes of Egypt" -- which preserves the Hebrew word order and YHWH as agent while being clear in English. LXX: *kai edoken kurios ten charin to lao autou enantion ton Aiguptio* -- adding *autou* ("his"), making explicit "his people."

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Kachatsot hallailah*: "about midnight"** -- Hebrew: *koh amar YHWH kachatsot hallailah ani yotse betokh Mitsrayim* (כֹּ֚ה אָמַ֣ר יְהוָ֔ה כַּחֲצֹ֣ת הַלַּ֔יְלָה אֲנִ֖י יוֹצֵ֣א בְתוֹךְ מִצְרָֽיִם). The phrase *kachatsot hallailah* uses *chatsot* from the root *ch-ts-h* ("to divide, to halve") -- midnight is the dividing-point of the night. The preposition *ke-* ("about, approximately") introduces deliberate imprecision: the exact moment is known only to YHWH. The declaration *ani yotse betokh Mitsrayim* ("I am going out into the midst of Egypt") uses the active participle *yotse* -- YHWH personally goes out, not an angel or agent. EMET: "about the middle of the night I [am] going out in the midst of Mitsrayim"; LOGOS: "about midnight I am going out into the midst of Egypt"; PNEUMA: "around midnight, I will go out through the heart of Egypt." The consensus follows LOGOS: "about midnight I am going out into the midst of Egypt" -- preserving both the approximation (*ke-*) and the participle (*yotse*). PNEUMA's "through the heart of" is interpretive; *betokh* means "in the midst of." LXX: *peri mesas nuktas ego eisporeuomai eis meson Aiguptou* -- note the LXX reverses the direction: "going into" rather than "going out."

> **[fn5] v.5 -- The firstborn merism: throne to millstones** -- Hebrew: *umet kol-bekhor be'erets Mitsrayim mibekhor Par'oh hayyoshev al-kis'o ad bekhor hashifkhah asher akhar harekh ayim vekhol bekhor behemah* (וּמֵ֣ת כָּל־בְּכוֹר֮ בְּאֶ֣רֶץ מִצְרַיִם֒ מִבְּכוֹר֤ פַּרְעֹה֙ הַיֹּשֵׁ֣ב עַל־כִּסְא֔וֹ עַ֚ד בְּכ֣וֹר הַשִּׁפְחָ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֖ר אַחַ֣ר הָרֵחָ֑יִם וְכֹ֖ל בְּכ֥וֹר בְּהֵמָֽה). The plague strikes across the entire social hierarchy: from the firstborn of Pharaoh "who sits upon his throne" (*hayyoshev al-kis'o*) -- the pinnacle of power -- to the firstborn of the maidservant "who is behind the millstones" (*asher akhar harekhayim*) -- the lowest social position. The *rekhayim* (dual form, "millstones") refers to the hand-mill, two grinding stones operated by the lowest-ranking household servants. The merism encompasses every social stratum. The extension *vekhol bekhor behemah* ("and every firstborn of the livestock") extends the judgment to the animal kingdom. EMET renders "slave-woman" for *shifkhah*; LOGOS renders "maidservant"; PNEUMA renders "slave-woman." The consensus follows LOGOS: "maidservant" -- the traditional English rendering of *shifkhah* that preserves the formal register. EMET and LOGOS both render "the mill" or "the millstones" for *harekhayim*; the consensus follows "the millstones" to preserve the Hebrew dual form. PNEUMA renders "the hand-mill" -- a reasonable interpretation but adds a modifier not in the text. For *behemah*, EMET renders "cattle," LOGOS renders "beast," PNEUMA renders "livestock." The consensus renders "livestock" -- *behemah* is a general term for domesticated animals, and "livestock" captures this better than the narrower "cattle" (EMET) or the archaic "beast" (LOGOS). LXX: *apo prototoku Pharao... heos prototoku tes therapaines tes para ton mulon* -- using *para* ("beside") rather than "behind."

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Tse'aqah gedolah*: "a great cry"** -- Hebrew: *vehayetah tse'aqah gedolah bekhol-erets Mitsrayim asher kamohu lo nihyatah vekhamohu lo tosif* (וְהָֽיְתָ֛ה צְעָקָ֥ה גְדֹלָ֖ה בְּכָל־אֶ֣רֶץ מִצְרָ֑יִם אֲשֶׁ֤ר כָּמֹ֨הוּ֙ לֹ֣א נִהְיָ֔תָה וְכָמֹ֖הוּ לֹ֥א תֹסִֽף). All three translators agree on "great cry" for *tse'aqah gedolah*. The word *tse'aqah* ("cry, outcry, shriek") is the same used for Israel's cry under slavery (2:23; 3:7, 9) -- the reversal is theologically deliberate. The oppressor's cry now mirrors the cry of the oppressed. The uniqueness formula ("such as has never been and such as shall never be again") echoes the formulae for the hail (9:18, 24) and locusts (10:14), but here it is human grief, not natural phenomenon, that is unprecedented. EMET: "which like it has not been, and like it shall not again be"; LOGOS: "such as has never been and such as shall never be again"; PNEUMA: "a cry such as has never been heard before and will never be heard again." PNEUMA adds "heard" -- reasonable but not in the Hebrew, which uses *nihyatah* ("been," not "been heard"). The consensus follows LOGOS for the most natural English rendering that preserves the Hebrew's verb. LXX: *estai krauge megale kata pasan gen Aiguptou* -- *krauge* ("cry, scream") for *tse'aqah*.

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Lo yecheratz kelev leshono*: "not a dog shall sharpen its tongue" and *yafleh*: "makes a distinction"** -- Hebrew: *ulekhol benei Yisra'el lo yecheratz-kelev leshono leme'ish ve'ad-behemah lema'an ted'un ki yafleh YHWH bein Mitsrayim uvein Yisra'el* (וּלְכֹ֣ל בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל לֹ֤א יֶֽחֱרַץ־כֶּ֨לֶב֙ לְשֹׁנ֔וֹ לְמֵאִ֖ישׁ וְעַד־בְּהֵמָ֑ה לְמַ֨עַן֙ תֵּֽדְע֔וּן כִּ֚י יַפְלֶ֣ה יְהוָ֔ה בֵּ֥ין מִצְרַ֖יִם וּבֵ֥ין יִשְׂרָאֵֽל). Two key phrases converge here. First, the dog proverb: *lo yecheratz-kelev leshono* -- the verb *yecheratz* from *charats* ("to sharpen, to cut, to decide") with *kelev* ("dog") and *leshono* ("its tongue") produces the image of a dog sharpening or pointing its tongue -- i.e., barking, snarling, or growling. The proverb means absolute, supernatural peace: while Egypt shrieks in anguish, not even a dog will disturb Israel's tranquility. EMET: "not a dog shall sharpen/move its tongue"; LOGOS: "not a dog shall sharpen its tongue"; PNEUMA: "not even a dog will snarl." The consensus follows LOGOS/EMET: "not a dog shall sharpen its tongue" -- preserving the Hebrew idiom. PNEUMA's "snarl" interprets the idiom but loses the vivid image of the tongue being sharpened. Second, the verb *yafleh* (hiphil of *palah*, "to make distinct, to separate"): "YHWH makes a distinction." All three translators agree on "makes a distinction." This is the climax of the *haflayah* (distinction) motif running through the plague narrative (8:22, 9:4, 9:26, 10:23). The doubled preposition *bein... uvein* ("between... and between") is preserved in the consensus -- the Hebrew insists on the preposition before both objects. LXX: *ou gruxei kuon te glosse autou* ("not a dog shall growl with its tongue") -- *gruzo* ("growl, grumble") for *yecheratz*.

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Bachari-af*: "in burning anger"** -- Hebrew: *veyardu khol-avadekha elleh elai vehishtakhavu-li lemor tse attah vekhol-ha'am asher-beraglekha ve'akharei-khen etse vayyetse me'im-Par'oh bakhari-af* (וְיָרְד֣וּ כָל־עֲבָדֶ֣יךָ אֵ֠לֶּה אֵלַ֞י וְהִשְׁתַּחֲווּ־לִ֣י לֵאמֹ֗ר צֵ֤א אַתָּה֙ וְכָל־הָעָ֣ם אֲשֶׁר־בְּרַגְלֶ֔יךָ וְאַחֲרֵי־כֵ֖ן אֵצֵ֑א וַיֵּצֵ֥א מֵֽעִם־פַּרְעֹ֖ה בׇּחֳרִי־אָֽף). Moses predicts that Pharaoh's own courtiers (*avadekha*, "your servants") will come to him and prostrate themselves (*hishtakhavu*, hitpael of *chavah*, "to bow down") -- a stunning reversal of court protocol. Moses conspicuously avoids saying Pharaoh himself will come -- diplomatic restraint even in rage. The phrase *asher-beraglekha* ("who are at your feet") = those who follow you. The critical phrase *bakhari-af* ("in burning anger"): *khari* from *kharah* ("to burn, be kindled"), *af* = "nose, anger." This is the only time Moses displays personal anger in the plague narrative. EMET: "in the heat of anger"; LOGOS: "in hot anger"; PNEUMA: "burning with anger" (restructured: "Moses stormed out... burning with anger"). The consensus renders "in burning anger" -- combining elements of all three: "burning" preserves the root *kharah* (PNEUMA), while the prepositional structure "in burning anger" preserves the Hebrew construct *bakhari-af* (EMET/LOGOS). PNEUMA's restructuring ("Moses stormed out") adds a verb ("stormed") not in the Hebrew and eliminates the Hebrew's syntactic construction. The Hebrew simply says *vayyetse me'im-Par'oh* ("he went out from Pharaoh"), which the consensus preserves literally. LXX: *exelthen de Mouses apo Pharao meta thumou* ("Moses went out from Pharaoh with rage") -- *thumos* ("fury, wrath") for *khari-af*.

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Revot mofetai*: "so that my wonders may be multiplied"** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh lo-yishma aleikhem Par'oh lema'an revot mofetai be'erets Mitsrayim* (וַיֹּ֤אמֶר יְהוָה֙ אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֔ה לֹא־יִשְׁמַ֤ע אֲלֵיכֶם֙ פַּרְעֹ֔ה לְמַ֛עַן רְב֥וֹת מוֹפְתַ֖י בְּאֶ֥רֶץ מִצְרָֽיִם). All three translators agree on the core rendering. The infinitive *revot* (from *ravah*, "to be many, to multiply") with *mofetai* ("my wonders") articulates divine purpose: Pharaoh's refusal serves the multiplication of wonders. The noun *mofet* ("wonder, portent") is distinct from *ot* ("sign") -- *mofet* emphasizes the spectacular, awe-inspiring quality. The purpose clause *lema'an* ("so that") states divine teleology directly. EMET: "so that my moftim [wonders] may be multiplied"; LOGOS: "so that My wonders may be multiplied"; PNEUMA: "so that my wonders may be multiplied." The consensus follows the shared rendering with lowercase "my" per SOPHIA convention (no capitalized divine pronouns). LXX: *hina plethunthosin mou ta semeia kai ta terata* ("so that my signs and wonders may be multiplied") -- the LXX characteristically doubles *mofetai* into "signs and wonders."

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Vaychazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh*: "YHWH strengthened the heart of Pharaoh" -- FIFTH DIVINE HARDENING** -- Hebrew: *uMosheh ve'Aharon asu et-kol-hamoftim ha'elleh lifnei Par'oh vaychazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh velo-shillakh et-benei-Yisra'el me'artso* (וּמֹשֶׁ֣ה וְאַהֲרֹ֗ן עָשׂ֛וּ אֶת־כָּל־הַמֹּפְתִ֥ים הָאֵ֖לֶּה לִפְנֵ֣י פַרְעֹ֑ה וַיְחַזֵּ֤ק יְהוָה֙ אֶת־לֵ֣ב פַּרְעֹ֔ה וְלֹֽא־שִׁלַּ֥ח אֶת־בְּנֵֽי־יִשְׂרָאֵ֖ל מֵאַרְצֽוֹ). **HARDENING VERB: chazaq (חזק), Piel וַיְחַזֵּק -- YHWH is the AGENT.** This is the fifth divine hardening (after 9:12, 10:1, 10:20, 10:27) and the capstone of the entire plague cycle. The piel form *vaychazzeq* indicates intensive, causative action -- YHWH actively and forcefully strengthened Pharaoh's heart. The verse functions as a closing frame for the plague narrative (chapters 7-11): despite all the wonders, YHWH hardened Pharaoh and he did not release Israel. The addition *me'artso* ("from his land") is emphatic -- the refusal is now territorial. All three translators agree on "strengthened" for *chazaq*. EMET: "YHWH chizzeq [strengthened] the heart of Par'oh"; LOGOS: "the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh"; PNEUMA: "YHWH had strengthened Pharaoh's heart." PNEUMA uses the pluperfect ("had strengthened"), interpreting the verse as a retrospective summary. While this interpretation has merit, the Hebrew wayyiqtol *vaychazzeq* is standard narrative sequence, and the consensus preserves the simple past: "YHWH strengthened the heart of Pharaoh." Rendered in bold and blockquoted to mark divine hardening agency. LXX: *esklerunen de kurios ten kardian Pharao* ("and the Lord hardened the heart of Pharaoh") -- *skleruno* ("harden") for *chazaq*.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### 1. Infinitive Absolute: *Garesh Yegaresh* (v. 1) -- Preserving the Hebrew Emphasis Pattern

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he will surely drive you out" | Literal: adverb "surely" for infinitive absolute |
| LOGOS | "he will surely drive you out" | Formal: same approach |
| PNEUMA | "he will drive you out completely, as one drives out an unwanted guest" | Dynamic: adds interpretive simile |

The SOPHIA convention requires preserving the infinitive absolute emphasis pattern. Both EMET and LOGOS collapse the Hebrew doubling into the adverb "surely," which is the traditional English approach but flattens the Hebrew syntax. PNEUMA captures the force but adds an interpretive simile ("as one drives out an unwanted guest") absent from the text. The consensus renders "driving, he will drive you out" -- the English participial form mirrors the Hebrew structure (*garesh yegaresh*) and preserves the intensification without adding to the text. This rendering is consistent with how SOPHIA has handled infinitive absolute constructions elsewhere.

### 2. *Sha'al* (v. 2) -- Full Consensus: "Ask" (Not "Borrow")

All three translators unanimously render *sha'al* as "ask" rather than "borrow." This is one of the rare cases of total agreement across all three approaches. The verb *sha'al* means "to ask, to request, to inquire" -- it carries no implication of return. The KJV's "borrow" has generated centuries of unnecessary theological difficulty (implying Israelite deception); the Hebrew simply describes a request. The Egyptian willingness to give (engineered by divine *chen* in v. 3) confirms the non-deceptive meaning. The consensus follows the unanimous rendering.

### 3. *Tse'aqah Gedolah* (v. 6) -- The Great Cry and Its Echo

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "which like it has not been, and like it shall not again be" | Literal: preserves Hebrew word order |
| LOGOS | "such as has never been and such as shall never be again" | Formal: natural English |
| PNEUMA | "a cry such as has never been heard before and will never be heard again" | Dynamic: adds "heard" |

All three agree on "great cry" for *tse'aqah gedolah*. The disagreement concerns the uniqueness formula. PNEUMA adds "heard" (*nihyatah* means "been," not "been heard"), importing a sensory detail not in the Hebrew. EMET's word order is stiff in English. The consensus follows LOGOS's rendering, which balances literalness with readability. The theological resonance -- Egypt's cry echoing Israel's cry (2:23, 3:7, 3:9) -- is preserved by using the same word "cry" rather than any substitute.

### 4. *Lo Yecheratz Kelev Leshono* (v. 7) -- Preserving the Dog Proverb

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not a dog shall sharpen/move its tongue" | Literal: dual rendering |
| LOGOS | "not a dog shall sharpen its tongue" | Formal: single verb |
| PNEUMA | "not even a dog will snarl" | Dynamic: interprets idiom |

The consensus preserves the Hebrew idiom: "not a dog shall sharpen its tongue." PNEUMA's "snarl" correctly interprets the meaning but loses the striking image of the tongue being sharpened -- a vivid proverb of absolute tranquility that should be available to the English reader. The LXX's *gruzo* ("growl") already interprets the idiom; the SOPHIA text preserves the Hebrew's concrete imagery. EMET's dual rendering ("sharpen/move") is unnecessary; "sharpen" alone captures *charats*.

### 5. *Bakhari-af* (v. 8) -- "Burning Anger" vs. "Hot Anger" vs. "Stormed Out"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in the heat of anger" | Literal: *khari* = "heat" |
| LOGOS | "in hot anger" | Formal: concise |
| PNEUMA | "Moses stormed out... burning with anger" | Dynamic: restructured, adds "stormed" |

The consensus renders "in burning anger" -- preserving the construct *bakhari-af* as a prepositional phrase while using "burning" to capture the root *kharah* ("to burn, be kindled"). LOGOS's "hot" is close but less vivid. EMET's "heat" is accurate but "burning" is more dynamic as an English modifier. PNEUMA's restructuring adds the verb "stormed" (not in the Hebrew) and breaks the sentence structure. The Hebrew *vayyetse me'im Par'oh* ("he went out from Pharaoh") is a simple narrative verb; the anger is described by the prepositional phrase, not by the verb of motion.

### 6. *Maidservant* vs. *Slave-woman* (v. 5) -- Register and Convention

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "slave-woman" | Literal: emphasizes social status |
| LOGOS | "maidservant" | Formal: traditional register |
| PNEUMA | "slave-woman" | Dynamic: modern social clarity |

The consensus follows LOGOS: "maidservant." While EMET and PNEUMA's "slave-woman" is more sociologically direct, *shifkhah* in Hebrew has a broader semantic range than modern "slave" and is traditionally rendered "maidservant" in English Bible translation. The word's full social implications are conveyed by the phrase "who is behind the millstones" -- the grinding labor makes the low social status unmistakable regardless of whether *shifkhah* is rendered "maidservant" or "slave-woman."

### 7. Recurring Convention Disagreements

**a. Divine name:** LOGOS uses "the LORD"; EMET/PNEUMA use YHWH. SOPHIA follows YHWH per established convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**b. Pronoun capitalization for God:** LOGOS capitalizes divine pronouns (My, Mine, He, Me); EMET and PNEUMA do not. SOPHIA follows lowercase convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**c. "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites":** EMET: "sons of Yisra'el"; LOGOS: "sons of Israel"; PNEUMA: "Israelites." SOPHIA follows "sons of Israel" per convention where the Hebrew reads *benei Yisra'el*. (Recurring, LOW)

**d. Hebrew names:** EMET uses transliterated forms (Mosheh, Par'oh, Aharon, Mitsrayim); LOGOS and PNEUMA use standard English forms (Moses, Pharaoh, Aaron, Egypt). SOPHIA follows standard English forms per convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**e. Tense framing:** PNEUMA uses pluperfect tense in vv. 1, 9, 10 ("YHWH had said," "YHWH had strengthened") to convey temporal retrospection. EMET and LOGOS use simple past. SOPHIA follows simple past for wayyiqtol verbs per convention. (Recurring, MEDIUM)

## Hardening-Verb Tracking -- Exodus 11

| Verse | Hebrew Root | Form | SOPHIA Rendering | Agent | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **11:10** | **chazaq (חזק)** | **Piel וַיְחַזֵּק** | **"YHWH strengthened the heart of Pharaoh"** | **YHWH** | **Capstone of plague cycle; fifth divine hardening** |

**Pattern note -- CAPSTONE HARDENING:** Exodus 11:10 closes the plague-announcement sequence (chapters 7-11). It pairs with v. 9 to form a two-verse theological summary: YHWH said Pharaoh would not listen (v. 9), and indeed YHWH strengthened Pharaoh's heart and he did not send out the sons of Israel (v. 10). The piel form of *chazaq* with YHWH as subject has now appeared four times (9:12, 10:20, 10:27, 11:10). The total divine hardening count stands at five (adding 10:1, *kaved* hiphil). After this, the next hardening will appear at 14:4, 8 -- at the Sea of Reeds -- where the hardening drives Pharaoh to pursue the departed Israelites to his own destruction.

### Cumulative Hardening-Verb Tracker -- Exodus 4-11

| # | Verse | Root | Form | SOPHIA Rendering | Agent |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4:21 | chazaq | Hiphil (prospective) | "strengthen" | YHWH (announced) |

**Phase analysis across Exodus 4-11:**

| Phase | Verses | Description | Agent |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1: Announcement** | 4:21, 7:3 | YHWH predicts he will harden Pharaoh | YHWH (future) |
| **Phase 2: Self-hardening** | 7:13, 7:22, 8:15, 8:19, 8:32, 9:7 | Pharaoh's heart strengthens itself or Pharaoh makes it heavy | Pharaoh / Heart |
| **Phase 3: Turning point** | 9:12 | YHWH strengthens Pharaoh's heart for the first time (chazaq piel) | YHWH |
| **Phase 4: Mixed agency** | 9:34, 9:35 | Pharaoh hardens himself again after divine hardening; heart is strengthened | Pharaoh + Heart |
| **Phase 5: Total divine control** | 10:1, 10:20, 10:27, 11:10 | Every hardening is divine; YHWH claims both roots (kaved + chazaq) | YHWH exclusively |

The trajectory reaches its terminus. The plague cycle that began with Pharaoh choosing to harden his own heart (Phase 2) ends with YHWH as the exclusive agent of hardening (Phase 5). The next chapter -- the Passover legislation of Exodus 12 -- will break the pattern: the death of the firstborn will accomplish what no amount of hardening could prevent. The man who would not send will be driven to send; the one who would not release will be compelled to expel.

*Consensus completed for Exodus 11:1-10 (10 verses). All readings follow the Leningrad Codex. LXX variants noted where significant (especially the directional reversal in v. 4 and the "signs and wonders" doubling in v. 9). One plague announced: the death of the firstborn (vv. 4-8). One divine hardening marked (v. 10) -- YHWH as agent, closing the plague cycle. Key renderings: "driving, he will drive you out" (v. 1, infinitive absolute preserved), "ask" (v. 2, unanimous against "borrow"), "not a dog shall sharpen its tongue" (v. 7, Hebrew idiom preserved), "makes a distinction" (v. 7, yafleh preserved), "in burning anger" (v. 8, bakhari-af). The cumulative hardening-verb tracker now records 16 instances across Exodus 4-11 (including 2 prospective announcements and 14 narrative enactments). The five-phase hardening structure is complete through its capstone.*

--- SOPHIA
